name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ كُونُوا۟ قَوَّٰمِينَ لِلَّهِ شُهَدَآءَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۖ وَلَا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شَنَـَٔانُ قَوْمٍ عَلَىٰٓ أَلَّا تَعْدِلُوا۟ ۚ ٱعْدِلُوا۟ هُوَ أَقْرَبُ لِلتَّقْوَىٰ ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (٨)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Be maintainers, as witnesses for the sake of Allah, of justice, and ill feeling for a people should never lead you to be unfair. Be fair; that is nearer to Godwariness, and be wary of Allah. Allah is indeed well aware of what you do. (8)
```

```arabic
وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ ۙ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ (٩)
```

```english
Allah has promised those who have faith and do righteous deeds forgiveness and a great reward. (9)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (١٠)
```

```english
As for those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of hell. (10)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ هَمَّ قَوْمٌ أَن يَبْسُطُوٓا۟ إِلَيْكُمْ أَيْدِيَهُمْ فَكَفَّ أَيْدِيَهُمْ عَنكُمْ ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (١١)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Remember Allah’s blessing upon you when a people set out to extend their hands against you, but He withheld their hands from you, and be wary of Allah, and in Allah let all the faithful put their trust. (11)
```

```arabic
۞ وَلَقَدْ أَخَذَ ٱللَّهُ مِيثَـٰقَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ وَبَعَثْنَا مِنْهُمُ ٱثْنَىْ عَشَرَ نَقِيبًا ۖ وَقَالَ ٱللَّهُ إِنِّى مَعَكُمْ ۖ لَئِنْ أَقَمْتُمُ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتَيْتُمُ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَءَامَنتُم بِرُسُلِى وَعَزَّرْتُمُوهُمْ وَأَقْرَضْتُمُ ٱللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا لَّأُكَفِّرَنَّ عَنكُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ وَلَأُدْخِلَنَّكُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۚ فَمَن كَفَرَ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ مِنكُمْ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ سَوَآءَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ (١٢)
```

```english
Certainly Allah took a pledge from the Children of Israel, and We raised among them twelve chiefs. And Allah said, ‘I am with you! Surely, if you maintain the prayer and give the zakat and have faith in My apostles and support them and lend Allah a good loan, I will surely absolve you of your misdeeds, and I will surely admit you into gardens with streams running in them. But whoever of you disbelieves after that has certainly strayed from the right way.’ (12)
```

```arabic
فَبِمَا نَقْضِهِم مِّيثَـٰقَهُمْ لَعَنَّـٰهُمْ وَجَعَلْنَا قُلُوبَهُمْ قَـٰسِيَةً ۖ يُحَرِّفُونَ ٱلْكَلِمَ عَن مَّوَاضِعِهِۦ ۙ وَنَسُوا۟ حَظًّا مِّمَّا ذُكِّرُوا۟ بِهِۦ ۚ وَلَا تَزَالُ تَطَّلِعُ عَلَىٰ خَآئِنَةٍ مِّنْهُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّنْهُمْ ۖ فَٱعْفُ عَنْهُمْ وَٱصْفَحْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٣)
```

```english
Then, because of their breaking their covenant We cursed them and made their hearts hard: they pervert words from their meanings, and have forgotten a part of what they were reminded. You will not cease to learn of some of their treachery, excepting a few of them. Yet excuse them and forbear. Indeed Allah loves the virtuous. (13)
```

```arabic
وَمِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا نَصَـٰرَىٰٓ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَـٰقَهُمْ فَنَسُوا۟ حَظًّا مِّمَّا ذُكِّرُوا۟ بِهِۦ فَأَغْرَيْنَا بَيْنَهُمُ ٱلْعَدَاوَةَ وَٱلْبَغْضَآءَ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۚ وَسَوْفَ يُنَبِّئُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَصْنَعُونَ (١٤)
```

```english
Also from those who say, ‘We are Christians,’ We took their pledge; but they forgot a part of what they were reminded. So We stirred up enmity and hatred among them until the Day of Resurrection, and soon Allah will inform them concerning what they had been doing. (14)
```

## (Commentary)

The connection between these verses is clear and beyond doubt; they constitute a series of addresses to the believers regarding the universal matters of their concern in both the Hereafter and this world, whether as individuals or as a community.

His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا كُونُوا قَوَّامِينَ لِلَّهِ شُهَداءَ بِالْقِسْطِ وَلا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شَنَآنُ قَوْمٍ عَلى أَلَّا تعدلوا** "O you who have faith! Be maintainers, as witnesses for the sake of Allah, with justice, and ill feeling for a people should never lead you to be unfair" (5:8). This verse is similar to the verse in *Surah al-Nisa’*: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا كُونُوا قَوَّامِينَ بِالْقِسْطِ شُهَداءَ لِلَّهِ وَلَوْ عَلى أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَوِ الْوالِدَيْنِ وَالْأَقْرَبِينَ إِنْ يَكُنْ غَنِيًّا أَوْ فَقِيراً فَاللهُ أَوْلى بِهِما فَلا تَتَّبِعُوا الْهَوى أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا وَإِنْ تَلْوُوا أَوْ تُعْرِضُوا فَإِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيراً** "O you who have faith! Be maintainers of justice and witnesses for the sake of Allah, even if it should be against yourselves or [your] parents and near relatives, and whether it be [someone] rich or poor, for Allah has a greater right over them both. So do not follow [your] desires, lest you should be unfair, and if you distort [your testimony] or disregard [it], Allah is indeed well aware of what you do" (4:135).

The difference between the two verses is that the verse in *al-Nisa’* is in the context of prohibiting deviation from justice in testimony due to following personal desire (اتباع الهوى)—where the witness favors the one for whom he testifies due to kinship or the like, thus testifying in a way that benefits them contrary to the truth. This verse—meaning the verse in *al-Ma’idah*—is in the context of deterring deviation from justice in testimony due to hatred (شنآن) and animosity from the witness toward the one against whom he testifies; thus, he gives testimony against him seeking a kind of revenge and the nullification of his right.

This difference in the objective of the discourse is what necessitated the variation of the qualifiers in the two verses. In the verse of *al-Nisa’*, He said: "Be maintainers of justice and witnesses for the sake of Allah," while in the verse of *al-Ma’idah*, He said: "Be maintainers, as witnesses for the sake of Allah, with justice."

This is because the objective in the verse of *al-Ma’idah*, being the deterrence from injustice in testimony due to prior enmity from the witness toward the one testified against, necessitated qualifying the testimony with justice (القسط). Thus, He commanded justice in testimony and that it should not involve injustice even against an enemy. This is in contrast to testifying for someone unjustly due to prior love and desire; such an act is not typically termed "injustice in testimony" or "deviation from justice" in common parlance, even if in reality it is not free from wrongdoing and bias. Therefore, in the verse of *al-Ma’idah*, He commanded testimony with justice (بالقسط) and made it a branch of the command to stand for Allah (القيام لله). In the verse of *al-Nisa’*, He commanded testimony for Allah (لله)—meaning that desire should not be followed therein—and made it a branch of the command to stand for justice (القيام بالقسط).

For this reason also, in the verse of *al-Ma’idah*, He branched from the command of testimony with justice His saying: **اعْدِلُوا هُوَ أَقْرَبُ لِلتَّقْوى وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ** "Be just; that is nearer to God-consciousness. And be wary of Allah" (5:8). Thus, He called to justice and considered it a means to attaining God-consciousness (التقوى) — *the inner state of godwariness that protects one from His displeasure*. He reversed the order in the verse of *al-Nisa’*, branching from the command of testimony for Allah His saying: **فَلا تَتَّبِعُوا الْهَوى أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا** "So do not follow [your] desires, lest you should be unfair" (4:135). Thus, He prohibited following desire and abandoning God-consciousness, considering it an evil means leading to the abandonment of justice.

Then, in both verses, He warned against the abandonment of God-consciousness with a single warning. In the verse of *al-Nisa’*, He said: "and if you distort [your testimony] or disregard [it], Allah is indeed well aware of what you do"—meaning if you are not God-conscious. In the verse of *al-Ma’idah*, He said: "And be wary of Allah; indeed Allah is well aware of what you do." As for the meaning of "maintainers for Allah, witnesses with justice," it has already been clarified in the discussion of the preceding verses.

His saying, the Exalted: **اعْدِلُوا هُوَ أَقْرَبُ لِلتَّقْوى** "Be just; that is nearer to God-consciousness" (5:8). The pronoun refers back to justice (العدل) as indicated by His saying: "Be just." The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ** "Allah has promised those who have faith and do righteous deeds that for them there will be forgiveness and a great reward" (5:9). The second sentence—meaning His saying: "for them there will be forgiveness"—is the performative instantiation (إنشاء) of the promise that He reported with His saying: "Allah has promised."

This is, as has been said, more emphatic in expression than His saying: **وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ مِنْهُمْ مَغْفِرَةً وَأَجْراً عَظِيماً** "Allah has promised those of them who have faith and do righteous deeds forgiveness and a great reward" (48:29). This is not, as some have claimed, because it is a report after a report—for that is an error—but rather because it is an explicit performative instantiation of the promise without it being merely implied, as is the case in the verse of *Surah al-Fath*.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَكَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ الْجَحِيمِ** "As for those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of hell" (5:10). Al-Raghib said: "The term *al-jahmah* (الجحمة) refers to the intensity of the fire's blazing, and from it comes *al-jahim* (الجحيم)." The verse contains the threat itself and stands in contrast to His saying in the previous verse: "for them there will be forgiveness and a great reward."

The qualification of disbelief (الكفر) with the denial of the signs (تكذيب الآيات) is to exclude that disbelief which is not accompanied by the denial of the indicative signs, and which does not reach the level of rejecting the truth while knowing it to be the truth—as in the case of the oppressed (الاستضعاف). In such a case, their matter is with Allah; if He wills, He forgives them, and if He wills, He punishes them. Thus, these two verses are a beautiful promise for those who believe and perform righteous deeds, and a severe threat for those who disbelieve and deny the signs of Allah. Between these two stages are intermediate stages and intervening stations whose matter and ultimate end Allah, the Glorified, has left ambiguous.

His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اذْكُرُوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ هَمَّ قَوْمٌ أَنْ يَبْسُطُوا** "O you who have faith! Remember Allah’s blessing upon you when a people intended to stretch out..." (5:11). This content is applicable to multiple and diverse events that occurred between the disbelievers and the Muslims, such as the battles of Badr, Uhud, al-Ahzab, and others. Thus, it appears that what is intended is the general intent of the polytheists to kill the believers and erase the traces of Islam and the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts*.

What some commentators have mentioned—that it refers to the intent of some polytheists to kill the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) or the intent of some Jews to assassinate him (the stories of which will follow)—is far from the apparent meaning of the wording, as is not hidden.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ وَعَلَى اللهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ** "And be wary of Allah, and in Allah let the faithful put their trust" (5:11). This is a command for God-consciousness and trust (التوكل) in Allah. In reality, it is a prohibition and a severe warning against abandoning God-consciousness and abandoning trust in Allah, the Glorified. The evidence for this is the narrative He recounted regarding the taking of the covenant (الميثاق) from the Children of Israel and from those who said, "We are Christians," followed by both groups breaking the divine covenant and Allah afflicting them with a curse, the hardening of hearts, and the forgetting of a portion of their religion, and the instigation of enmity and hatred among them until the Day of Resurrection.

He only mentioned the story to use it as a witness against the believers and to place it before their eyes so they might take heed and realize that the Jews and Christians were only afflicted with what they were afflicted with because of their forgetting the covenant of Allah, the Glorified. It was nothing other than a covenant of submission (الإسلام) to Allah, which they ratified with hearing and obedience. The necessary consequence of that was that they should be wary of opposing their Lord and that they should trust in Him in the matters of their religion—meaning they should take Him as a guardian (وكيل) therein, choosing what He chooses for them and abandoning what He dislikes for them. The path to this is the obedience of His messengers by believing in them and abandoning the following of anyone other than Allah and His messengers, such as those who call to themselves and demand submission to their command, like tyrants, transgressors, and others, even the rabbis and monks. For there is no obedience except to Allah or to those whose obedience He has commanded.

However, they cast it behind their backs, so they were distanced from the mercy of Allah and they distorted the words from their contexts, interpreting them in ways other than what was intended. This necessitated that they forgot a portion of the religion, which was a share and a portion whose departure took with it all goodness and felicity. This corrupted what remained in their hands of the religion, for religion is a composite of knowledge and laws, some of which are connected to others; the corruption of some leads to the corruption of the rest, especially the pillars and foundations (الأصول). This is like one who prays but not for the sake of Allah, or spends but not for the pleasure of Allah, or fights but not to exalt the word of truth.

Thus, what remained in their hands did not benefit them, as it was distorted and corrupt, nor were they able to do without what they had forgotten of the religion; for there is no self-sufficiency from religion, especially its foundations and pillars.

From this, it is known that the context requires warning the believers against opposing God-consciousness and abandoning trust in Allah by mentioning this story and calling them to take heed from it.

From this, it also becomes clear that what is intended by trust (التوكل) is that which encompasses both legislative (تشريعية) and ontological (تكوينية) matters, or what is specific to the legislative—in the sense that Allah, the Glorified, commands the believers to obey Allah and His Messenger in His religious laws and what His Messenger has brought and clarified for them, and to entrust the matter of religion and divine laws to their Lord. They must refrain from acting independently and from manipulating what He has entrusted to them of His laws, just as He commands them to obey Him in the system of causes and effects (الأسباب والمسببات) that He has established for them. They should proceed according to this system without relying upon it or granting it independence and lordship (ربوبية), while waiting for what Allah intends and chooses for them of results through His governance and will.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ أَخَذَ اللهُ مِيثاقَ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ وَبَعَثْنا مِنْهُمُ اثْنَيْ عَشَرَ نَقِيباً** "Allah certainly took a pledge from the Children of Israel, and We raised among them twelve chiefs..." (5:12). Al-Raghib said: "*Al-naqb* (النقب) in a wall or skin is like a hole in wood." He said: "And the *naqib* (النقيب) is the one who searches out a people and their conditions; its plural is *nuqaba’* (نقباء)."

Allah, the Glorified, recounts to the believers of this community what occurred with the Children of Israel regarding the strengthening of their religion and the stabilization of their affair through the taking of the covenant, the raising of the chiefs, the delivery of the clarification, and the completion of the proof (إتمام الحجة), followed by how they met this with the breaking of the covenant, and how Allah, the Glorified, met them with the curse and the hardening of hearts, and so on.

So He said: "Allah certainly took a pledge from the Children of Israel"—which is what He mentions frequently in *Surah al-Baqarah* and elsewhere—"and We raised among them twelve chiefs." It appears that they were the heads of the twelve tribes, acting as governors over them, managing their affairs. Their relationship to their tribes is, in a sense, like the relationship of the "holders of authority" (أولي الأمر) to the individuals in this community; they are the point of reference in matters of religion and the world, except that they do not receive revelation nor do they legislate a law, for that belongs only to Allah and His Messenger. "And Allah said, 'I am with you'"—an announcement of protection and observation. From this, it follows that He will help them if they obey Him and forsake them if they disobey Him. Therefore, He mentioned both matters together, saying: **لَئِنْ أَقَمْتُمُ الصَّلاةَ وَآتَيْتُمُ الزَّكاةَ وَآمَنْتُمْ بِرُسُلِي وَعَزَّرْتُمُوهُمْ** "If you maintain the prayer and give the zakat and believe in My apostles and support them..." (5:12). Support (التعزير) is assistance accompanied by veneration. What is intended by the "apostles" are those who would face them with their mission and call in the future, such as Jesus and Muhammad (عليه السلام) and others whom Allah sent between Moses and Muhammad (عليه السلام). **وَأَقْرَضْتُمُ اللهَ قَرْضاً حَسَناً** "and lend Allah a good loan"—which refers to recommended spending (الإنفاق المندوب) other than the obligatory zakat. **لَأُكَفِّرَنَّ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ وَلَأُدْخِلَنَّكُمْ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ** "I will surely absolve you of your misdeeds and I will surely admit you into gardens with conduits running amidst them" (5:12). This is what pertains to the beautiful promise. Then He said: **فَمَنْ كَفَرَ بَعْدَ ذلِكَ مِنْكُمْ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ سَواءَ السَّبِيلِ** "But whoever of you disbelieves after that has certainly strayed from the right way" (5:12).

His saying, the Exalted: **فَبِما نَقْضِهِمْ مِيثاقَهُمْ لَعَنَّاهُمْ وَجَعَلْنا قُلُوبَهُمْ قاسِيَةً** "So because of their breaking their pledge We cursed them and made their hearts hard" (5:13). He mentioned the recompense for disbelieving in the aforementioned covenant as straying from the right way (سواء السبيل). This is a summary mention which is detailed by the types of afflictions in this verse, some of which Allah, the Glorified, attributed to Himself—such as the curse and the hardening of hearts, where such attribution is appropriate—and some of which He attributed to themselves as occurring by their own choice, as He intends by His saying: **وَلا تَزالُ تَطَّلِعُ عَلى خائِنَةٍ مِنْهُمْ** "and you will not cease to learn of some treachery on their part" (5:13). All of this is their recompense for disbelieving in the signs of Allah, at the head of which was the covenant taken from them, or the recompense for their disbelief in the covenant specifically; for the "right way" from which they strayed is the path of felicity by which their world and their Hereafter are built.

So His saying: "So because of their breaking their pledge"—it appears that this is the disbelief for which Allah threatened them in the previous verse. The particle *ma* (ما) in His saying "So because of" (فبما) is for emphasis and provides a sense of ambiguity for the purpose of magnification, or belittlement, or otherwise. The meaning is: "So because of a certain breaking of their pledge by them, We cursed them." The curse (اللعن) is the distancing from mercy. "And We made their hearts hard (قاسية)." Hardness (قسوة) of the heart is derived from the hardness of stones, which is their solidity. A "hard" heart is one that does not humble itself before the truth nor is it affected by mercy. He, the Exalted, said: **أَلَمْ يَأْنِ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنْ تَخْشَعَ قُلُوبُهُمْ لِذِكْرِ اللهِ وَما نَزَلَ مِنَ الْحَقِّ وَلا يَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ مِنْ قَبْلُ فَطالَ عَلَيْهِمُ الْأَمَدُ فَقَسَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ وَكَثِيرٌ مِنْهُمْ فاسِقُونَ** "Is it not time yet for those who have faith that their hearts should be humbled for Allah’s remembrance and to the truth which has come down [to them], and that they should not be like those who were given the Book before? Time passed them by and so their hearts were hardened, and many of them are transgressors" (57:16).

In short, the consequence of their hearts' hardness was that they returned to **يُحَرِّفُونَ الْكَلِمَ عَنْ مَواضِعِهِ** "perverting the words from their contexts" (5:13) by interpreting them in ways that do not please Allah, the Glorified, and by omission, addition, or alteration; all of this is part of distortion (التحريف). This led them to miss clear realities of the religion: **وَنَسُوا حَظًّا مِمَّا ذُكِّرُوا بِهِ** "and they have forgotten a part of what they were reminded of" (5:13). This was nothing other than a portion of the foundations (الأصول) upon which felicity revolves, and nothing can take its place except that which seals upon them the necessary wretchedness—such as their belief in anthropomorphism (التشبيه), the finality of Moses' prophethood, the permanence of the Torah's law, and the invalidity of abrogation (النسخ) and divine revision (البداء), among other things.

"and you will not cease to learn of some treachery (خائنة) on their part"—meaning a treacherous group among them, or treachery from them—**إِلَّا قَلِيلاً مِنْهُمْ فَاعْفُ عَنْهُمْ وَاصْفَحْ إِنَّ اللهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُحْسِنِينَ** "except a few of them. Yet excuse them and forbear. Indeed Allah loves the virtuous" (5:13). It has been mentioned repeatedly that the exception of a few of them does not contradict the establishment of the curse and punishment for the collective, which is the people and the nation. (1)

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمِنَ الَّذِينَ قالُوا إِنَّا نَصارى أَخَذْنا مِيثاقَهُمْ فَنَسُوا حَظًّا مِمَّا ذُكِّرُوا بِهِ فَأَغْرَيْنا** "Also from those who say, ‘We are Christians,’ We took a pledge; but they forgot a part of what they were reminded of. So We stirred up..." (5:14). Al-Raghib said: "*Ghariya* (غري) with such-and-such" means he became attached to it and stuck to it. The origin of this is from *al-ghira’* (الغراء), which is glue. "I stirred up (أغريت) so-and-so with such-and-such" is like making him attached to it.

The Messiah, Jesus son of Mary, was a prophet of mercy who called people to reconciliation and peace, and urged them to look toward the Hereafter and turn away from the pleasures and ornaments of the world, and forbade them from competing for this lowly gain. (2) When they forgot a portion of what they were reminded of, Allah, the Glorified, established in their hearts war in place of peace and reconciliation, and replaced the brotherhood and affection to which they were invited with enmity and hatred, as He says: "but they forgot a part of what they were reminded of. So We stirred up enmity and hatred among them until the Day of Resurrection." This enmity and hatred which Allah, the Exalted, mentioned have become deeply rooted dispositions (ملكات) fixed among these Christian nations, like the fire of the Hereafter from which they have no escape; whenever they desire to emerge from its anguish, they are returned into it and told, "Taste the punishment of the burning."

Since Jesus son of Mary (عليه السلام) was raised, and his disciples and the traveling preachers among their students differed among themselves, discord broke out between them. It did not cease to grow and increase until it turned into wars, killings, raids, and various forms of displacement and expulsion, eventually leading to great world wars that threaten the earth with ruin and humanity with annihilation and extinction.

All of this is from the transformation of blessing into affliction and the production of straying from effort: **وَسَوْفَ يُنَبِّئُهُمُ اللهُ بِما كانُوا يَصْنَعُونَ** "and soon Allah will inform them of what they had been doing" (5:14).

---
(1) A strange opinion found in some commentaries is that the "few" refers to Abdullah ibn Salam and his companions, despite the fact that Abdullah ibn Salam had embraced Islam a long time before the revelation of this Surah. The apparent meaning of the verse is the exception of some Jews who had not yet embraced Islam at the time of the verse's revelation.
(2) Refer in this regard to the statements of the Messiah (عليه السلام) in his various positions narrated from him in the four Gospels.
